mainmatter.p: Had both my POPL 2024 submissions conditionally accepted.

frontmatter.title: decalf: a directed, effectful cost-aware logical framework
frontmatter.meta: Conditionally accepted, POPL ’24: 51st ACM SIGPLAN Symposium on Principles of Programming Languages

mainmatter.p: We present decalf, a directed, effectful cost-aware logical framework for studying quantitative aspects of functional programs with effects. Like calf, the language is based on a formal phase distinction between the extension and the intension of a program, its pure behavior as distinct from its cost measured by an effectful step-counting primitive. The type theory ensures that the behavior is unaffected by the cost accounting. Unlike calf, the present language takes account of effects, such as probabilistic choice and mutable state; this extension requires a reformulation of calf’s approach to cost accounting: rather than rely on a “separable” notion of cost, here a cost bound is simply another program. To make this formal, we equip every type with an intrinsic preorder, relaxing the precise cost accounting intrinsic to a program to a looser but nevertheless informative estimate. For example, the cost bound of a probabilistic program is itself a probabilistic program that specifies the distribution of costs. This approach serves as a streamlined alternative to the standard method of isolating a recurrence that bounds the cost in a manner that readily extends to higher-order, effectful programs.

The development proceeds by first introducing the decalf type system, which is based on an intrinsic ordering among terms that restricts in the extensional phase to extensional equality, but in the intensional phase reflects an approximation of the cost of a program of interest. This formulation is then applied to a number of illustrative examples, including pure and effectful sorting algorithms, simple probabilistic programs, and higher-order functions. Finally, we justify decalf via a model in the topos of augmented simplicial sets.

frontmatter.title: The essence of generalized algebraic data types
frontmatter.meta: Conditionally accepted, POPL ’24: 51st ACM SIGPLAN Symposium on Principles of Programming Languages

mainmatter.p: This paper considers direct encodings of generalized algebraic data types (GADTs) in a minimal suitable lambda-calculus. To this end, we develop an extension of System \text{F} _ \omega with recursive types and internalized type equalities with injective constant type constructors. We show how GADTs and associated pattern-matching constructs can be directly expressed in the calculus, thus showing that it may be treated as a highly idealized modern functional programming language. We prove that the internalized type equalities in conjunction with injectivity rules increase the expressive power of the calculus by establishing a non-macro-expressibility result in \text{F} _ \omega, and prove the system type-sound via a syntactic argument. Finally, we build two relational models of our calculus: a simple, unary model that illustrates a novel, two-stage interpretation technique, necessary to account for the equational constraints; and a more sophisticated, binary model that relaxes the construction to allow, for the first time, formal reasoning about data-abstraction in a calculus equipped with GADTs.
Finished reconstructing the paper version of impredicative subuniverses are reflective for strong impredicative encodings.

In regard to strong impredicative encodings, Mike Shulman answered my question from yesterday: apparently we can nonetheless split arbitrary quasi-idempotents in the wild category of A-algebras, and this makes essential use of the second component of the homotopy \mathsf{happly} \, Q_0 \colon m \circ m \sim m. It is really fascinating that the splitting that I constructed last time worked differently and seemed to take advantage of the specific cone involved; this clarity is a really nice consequence of Mike’s preference to focus on the more general result.

Today I finished my Agda formalization in TypeTopology of the wild-categorical results that Mike Shulman and I were hoping for in regard to strong impredicative encodings. Then I translated these proofs into English. I started trying to re-deduce impredicative subuniverses are reflective in this style, but noticed that there may be an issue: in the old proof, we split a very specific quasi-idempotent, but here I am trying to split them all. I wrote to Mike Shulman mentioning this to him.

Mike Shulman has sent me a potential proof of quasi-idempotence for the generic element of a semicoherent cone, in our strong impredicative encodings project. This relies on a “fattened” definition of semicoherence in the wild-categorical setting, similar to Mike’s fattened version in the special case of subuniverses. Mike points out that the condition, when specialized for cones over the identity functor, is much simpler than the general case. I am thinking that I might see if it is possible to formalize it in TypeTopology.
I finished my pass through towards a geometry for syntax and submitted the revision to the arXiv.
Today I added an experimental \iftex command to Forester 2.5 [unreleased] in response to Vikraman Choudhury’s post to the mailing list.

I started to resume my work on strong impredicative encodings in intensional type theory with Mike Shulman in terms of wild category theory. I ended up getting totally snowed under coherences and asked Mike Shulman for help.
My new spaces for denotational semantics grant was awarded by United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research.
